Fun Polrrrcrt, PRlcrtcrs CouvttssloN
P.O. Box lt07 . "121t J Street . Sacramento. CA f-illl2-0t107
(916) 322--s660 . Fax (916) -l2l-08tt6
August 17,2000
Richard L. Christenson
Christenson Law Firm
4T2WestPutnam Avenue
Porterville, California 93257
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-166
Dear Mr. Christenson
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
political Reform Act (the "Act").r Your client, Ron Irish, is a current member of the Porterville
city council, and you are authorized to request advice on his behalf.
QUESTION
May council member Irish vote on Frank's Stop N Go Market's application for a
conditional use permit allowing the market to sell beer and wine when the issue is considered by
the Porterville City Council?
CONCLUSION
No. Council member Irish may not participate in council votes on this permit because he
has received at least $250 from Frank's Stop N Go Market in the past year for private security
services and the decision to approve or deny the alcohol permit will have some financial effect
on Frank's Market.
' Goue--ent Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear attitle2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. A-00-166
Page2
FACTS 
2
Mr. Irish is the sole proprietor of Security Telecommunications of Porterville which
installs and monitors residential and commercial alarm systems throughout the greater Porterville
area. For at least the last fifteen years, Mr. Irish has provided security services to Frank's Stop N
Go Market located at 610 West Olive Avenue, Porterville, Califomia.
A fixed contract for services currently is in place which extends for an additional three-
year term. The contract terms are set and no intention exists on Mr. kish's part to modiff the
contract in any manner. The contract provides for monthly compensation of $30 per month or
annual charges of $360. Frank's Stop N Go Market is current on all existing charges.
An issue is pending currently before the Porterville City Council regarding Frank's Stop
N Go Market's application for a conditional use permit that would allow the sale of beer and
wine from the commercial site. To this point, Mr. kish has abstained from any voting citing a
potential conflict of interest based on his conhactual relationship with the market. The issue has
resulted in a split vote; two votes "aye" and two votes "no" from a five-person council.
With regard to conflicts of interest, Mr. kish is informed that there may be an annual
compensation threshold of $250, but has also been informed that compensation from a fixed
contract that extends for a set contractual term would not constitute a conflict of interest.
Mr. Irish would like to participate in the council discussion and voting as it relates to the
conditional use permit process. This is an issue of some consequence that has arisen on several
occasions before the council in the current term of office.
AN,{LYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
' Th" Co**ission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. This advice is applicable and
confers immunity (see Section 83 I 14) only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all of the
material facts have been disclose d. (ln re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 7 l, 77 .)


File No. A-00-166
Page 3
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard, eight-step analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision.3
(Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) The following advice applies that standard analysis.
A. Public official.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bX1).) As a member of the Porterville City Council, Mr. Irish
is a "public official" for purposes of the Act (see Sections 82048, 82041). Therefore, the
conflict-of-interest rules apply to him.
B. The Act's conflict-of-interest rules apply to this decision.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply where a public official "make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his official position to influence a
govemmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700(bX2).) In voting on the conditional use permit, Mr. Irish
would be making a governmental decision.
C. Identifying economic interests.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising fuom economic
interests. Identi$ing which, if any, of these economic interests are held by a public official is
the third step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest under the Act. (See Regulation
18700(bX3).) There are five kinds of such economic interests.
1. A public official has an economic interest in abusiness entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in
which he or she is a director, fficer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
managemenr (Section 87 I 03(d); Regulation 1 8703. I (b));
2. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
3 The seventh step consists of determining whether the "public generally" exception applies. (Regulation
18707.) Based on the facts you have provided, this exception does not apply.


File No. A-00-166
Page 4
3. A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, includingpromised
income, which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
871 03(c); Regulation I 8703.3);
4. A public official has an economic interest in any source of Stfts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4);
5. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family-this is known as the
"personal financial effects" rule (Section 87I03; Regulation 18703.5).
In this case, Frank's Stop N Go Market has been a source of income to council member
Irish of at least $250 within the past 12 months, so council member kish has an economic
interest in Frank's Market.
D. Determining whether the public official's economic interest is directly or
indirectly involved in the governmental decision.
Once it has been determined that a public official has an economic interest in a source of
income, the next step is determining the degree to which the economic interest is involved in the
governmental decision in question. (Regulation 18700(b)( ).)
A business entity, a source of income, or a source of gifts is directly involved in a
decision when that person:
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 1870a.1(a)(l));
Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official's agency. A source of income is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
(Regulation I 87 0 a.l @)(2).)
Under the Commission's regulations, sources of income which are not directly involved
under the rules stated above are considercd indirectly involved for purposes of choosing a
materiality standard. (Regulation 1 8704. 1(b).)
Here, Frank's Stop N Go Market, a source of income to council member Irish, has filed
the application for a conditional use permit that would allow it to sell beer and wine, so Frank's
Market is directly involved in the council decision to approve or deny the use permit.
o
a


File No. A-00-166
Page 5
E. Materiality standard.
Under Regulation 18705.3(a), if a source of income is directly tnvolved in a governmental
decision, as Frank's Market is, then any reasonablyforeseeablefinancial effect of the decision
on the source of income is deemed material.
F. Using the materiality standards to decide if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect.
The next step, in deciding whether an official has a conflict of interest is using the
materiality standards to decide if amaterial financial effect on one of more of the official's
economic interests is reasonably foreseeable as a result of the decision. (Regulation 18706.)
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706 In re
Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
In this case, it is substantially likely that a council decision to grant or deny Frank's
Market's application for a permit to sell alcohol will have some financial effect on the market.
This being the case, council member Irish has a conflict of interest and may not participate in the
vote on the permit application. You state that Mr. hish has a fixed contract with Frank's Market
for providing security systems, and the amount of income he receives from this contract will not
be affected by the cour.cil vote on the permit. There is, however, no exception in the Act or
regulations under which the income received by Mr. Irish from Frank's Market does not give rise
to a disqualifying conflict of interest for him in this situation.
G. Legally required participation.
The last step, which pertains to the "legally required participation" rule (see Regulation
18708), applies only in rare cases where several public officials in the same agency are
simultaneously disqualified. In such cases, legally required participation may apply to permit an
official who would otherwise be disqualified from a decision to vote, if a quorum cannot be
reached without the disqualified official and there is no alternate source of decisionmaking.
However, legally required participation specifically does not apply to permit an official with a
conflict to vote in order to break a tie, or to convene a quorum on a body if there are suffrcient
other members of the agency who are not disqualified but simply are not present at a particular
meeting. (Regulation 18708.) Hence, the legally required participation rule does not apply here.


File No. A-00-166
Page 6
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-s660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

the Porterville City Council?

on Frank's Market.
' Goue--ent Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear attitle2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May council member Irish vote on Frank's Stop N Go Market's application for a
conditional use permit allowing the market to sell beer and wine when the issue is considered by
the Porterville City Council?

CONCLUSION: No. Council member Irish may not participate in council votes on this permit because he
has received at least $250 from Frank's Stop N Go Market in the past year for private security
services and the decision to approve or deny the alcohol permit will have some financial effect
on Frank's Market.
' Goue--ent Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear attitle2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
